project_title_en: Old Age Security Extended Absence Leave Prioritization
description:
Service Canada is responsible for investigating misuse or abuse of the Old
Age Security (OAS) program including, but not limited to, identifying
individuals who do not qualify to receive OAS while outside of Canada and
stopping payment after a 6 month absence, as per the program’s policy. This
also applies to individuals receiving the Guaranteed Income Supplement,
which cannot be collected if they are outside Canada for more than 6 months.
To assist in those responsibilities, Service Canada receives traveller
information from the Canada Border Service Agency (CBSA), which is then
compared against OAS claimants to identify individuals who have failed to
report or have misreported their absence from Canada.
A system was designed to identify misuse or abuse of the OAS program by
applying techniques of case load prioritization and optimization with the
objective to focus on priority cases. The system does not render decisions
regarding the recipient’s eligibility. The decision to investigate an identified
individual and render any administrative decision is performed by a human
investigator. The proceeding investigations will be conducted thoroughly, and
individuals identified are given an opportunity to present the necessary
documentation and explanation prior to the investigators producing any
decisions.
The system uses a self-organizing map and clustering model applied to data
from the CBSA traveller information system, OAS pensions system, and SIN
registry. This project has been conducted with oversight from the Artificial
Intelligence Centre of Excellence in accordance with the Treasury Board of
Canada Secretariat guidelines.
Legal services were engaged to review the project under the terms of the
Directive on Automated Decision-Making Systems which took effect on April
1, 2020.
qa_section_text:
Section 3.1: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation
1. What is motivating your team to introduce automation into this decision-making process?
(Check all that apply)
Improve overall quality of decisions
Existing backlog of work or cases
The system is performing tasks that humans could not accomplish in a
reasonable period of time
2. What client needs will the system address and how will this system meet them? If possible,
describe how client needs have been identified.
The system aims to reduce the complexity of the decisions Integrity
Operations agents are required to make by prioritizing the cases that require
immediate inspection. The system will automate a triaging function by
employing data analytics to identify prioritized cases. The system will
categorize cases based on established program eligibility criteria by analyzing
entry and exit data supplied by CBSA and ESDC program data. Without this
solution, the agents will be faced with a backlog of cases that may not require
immediate inspection, and further delay the processing of priority cases.
3. Please describe any public benefits the system is expected to have.
This system will optimize the inspection procedures by reducing processing
times, streamlining the inspection/assessment process, saving costs on an
otherwise time consuming process. The solution will increase the ability of
the department to ensure the integrity of the program.
4. How effective will the system likely be in meeting client needs?
Moderately effective [ Points: +1 ]
5. Please describe any improvements, benefits, or advantages you expect from using an
automated system. This could include relevant program indicators and performance targets.
The solution will increase the ability of the department to ensure the integrity
of the program. The system would be able to efficiently categorize cases
based on a number of criteria including (1) length of recipient’s absence from
Canada, (2) state of recipient (alive or deceased), (3) status of recipient’s OAS
benefts (i.e.: active, ineligible, etc), highlighting the ones requiring inspection.
The system will support agents by providing them with a list of cases that
require immediate action by filtering the cases based on the set program
criteria and grouping them.
6. Please describe how you will ensure that the system is confined to addressing the client
needs identified above.
This system will not autonomously make decisions. Instead, it will use the
program criteria to categorize the cases based on priority. The Integrity
Operations agent would then conduct a thorough pre-assessment before
beginning an inspection.
7. Please describe any trade-offs between client interests and program objectives that you have
considered during the design of the project.
The purpose of this system is to enhance the integrity of the OAS program by
supporting agents in making decisions around the eligibility of clients
receiving OAS (and other non-portable benefits such as GIS). To ensure that
this system does not disproportionately target at-risk clients, the system will
undergo an ethical review and fairness assessment.
8. Have alternative non-automated processes been considered?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
9. If non-automated processes were considered, why was automation identified as the preferred
option?
This solution will enhance the integrity of the program by optimizing the
process and delivering improved outcomes. The solution will increase
efficiencies by lowering resource costs, reducing processing times, improving
accuracy of benefits distribution by identifying ineligible recipients, and
thereby improve stewardship of the program’s financial integrity.
10. What would be the consequence of not deploying the system?
Service quality is not as high
Risk Profile
11. Is the project within an area of intense public scrutiny (e.g. because of privacy concerns)
and/or frequent litigation?
No [ Points: +0 ]
12. Are clients in this line of business particularly vulnerable?
Yes [ Points: +3 ]
13. Are stakes of the decisions very high?
No [ Points: +0 ]
14. Will this project have major impacts on staff, either in terms of their numbers or their roles?
No [ Points: +0 ]
15. Will the use of the system create or exacerbate barriers for persons with disabilities?
No [ Points: +0 ]
Project Authority
16. Will you require new policy authority for this project?
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Algorithm
17. The algorithm used will be a (trade) secret
No [ Points: +0 ]
18. The algorithmic process will be difficult to interpret or to explain
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Decision
19. Please describe the decision(s) that will be automated.
The system will filter the data based on the program criteria and summarize
the data into groups with a grouping label and provide a summary by group.
This will help facilitate the prioritization of cases that need immediate action.
20. Does the decision pertain to any of the categories below (check all that apply):
Social assistance

Algorithmic Impact Assessment Results
Version: 0.10.0
Project Details
1. Name of Respondent
Adam Kowalczewski

Senior Business Analyst

5. Project Title
Old Age Security Extended Absence Leave Prioritization
6. Project ID from IT Plan
Lite-2019-054
7. Departmental Program (from Department Results Framework)
Pensions and Benefits
8. Project Phase
Implementation [ Points: 0 ]
9. Please provide a project description:
Service Canada is responsible for investigating misuse or abuse of the Old
Age Security (OAS) program including, but not limited to, identifying
individuals who do not qualify to receive OAS while outside of Canada and
stopping payment after a 6 month absence, as per the program’s policy. This
also applies to individuals receiving the Guaranteed Income Supplement,
which cannot be collected if they are outside Canada for more than 6 months.
To assist in those responsibilities, Service Canada receives traveller
information from the Canada Border Service Agency (CBSA), which is then
compared against OAS claimants to identify individuals who have failed to
report or have misreported their absence from Canada.
A system was designed to identify misuse or abuse of the OAS program by
applying techniques of case load prioritization and optimization with the
objective to focus on priority cases. The system does not render decisions
regarding the recipient’s eligibility. The decision to investigate an identified
individual and render any administrative decision is performed by a human
investigator. The proceeding investigations will be conducted thoroughly, and
individuals identified are given an opportunity to present the necessary
documentation and explanation prior to the investigators producing any
decisions.
The system uses a self-organizing map and clustering model applied to data
from the CBSA traveller information system, OAS pensions system, and SIN
registry. This project has been conducted with oversight from the Artificial
Intelligence Centre of Excellence in accordance with the Treasury Board of
Canada Secretariat guidelines.
Legal services were engaged to review the project under the terms of the
Directive on Automated Decision-Making Systems which took effect on April
1, 2020.
About The System
10. Please check which of the following capabilities apply to your system.
Process optimization and workflow automation: Analyzing large data sets to
identify and anomalies, cluster patterns, predict outcomes or ways to
optimize; and automate specific workflows
Section 1: Impact Level : 1
Current Score: 31
Raw Impact Score: 31
Mitigation Score: 25
Section 2: Requirements Specific to Impact Level 1
Peer review
None
Gender-based Analysis Plus
None
Notice
None
Human-in-the-loop for decisions
Decisions may be rendered without direct human involvement.
Explanation
In addition to any applicable legal requirement, ensure that a meaningful explanation is published
for common decision results. The explanation must provide a general description of:
the role of the system in the decision-making process;
input data, its source and method of collection;
the criteria used to evaluate input data and the operations applied to process it;
the output produced by the system and any relevant information needed to interpret it in the
context of the administrative decision; and
the principal factors behind a decision.
Explanations must also inform clients of relevant recourse options, where appropriate.
Descriptions must be made available in plain language through the Algorithmic Impact
Assessment and discoverable via a departmental website.
Training
None
IT and business continuity management
None
Approval for the system to operate
None
Other requirements
The Directive on Automated Decision-Making also includes other requirements that must be met
for all impact levels.
Directive on Automated Decision-Making
Contact your institution's ATIP office to discuss the requirement for a Privacy Impact
Assessment as per the Directive on Privacy Impact Assessment.
Section 3: Questions and Answers
Section 3.1: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation
1. What is motivating your team to introduce automation into this decision-making process?
(Check all that apply)
Improve overall quality of decisions
Existing backlog of work or cases
The system is performing tasks that humans could not accomplish in a
reasonable period of time
2. What client needs will the system address and how will this system meet them? If possible,
describe how client needs have been identified.
The system aims to reduce the complexity of the decisions Integrity
Operations agents are required to make by prioritizing the cases that require
immediate inspection. The system will automate a triaging function by
employing data analytics to identify prioritized cases. The system will
categorize cases based on established program eligibility criteria by analyzing
entry and exit data supplied by CBSA and ESDC program data. Without this
solution, the agents will be faced with a backlog of cases that may not require
immediate inspection, and further delay the processing of priority cases.
3. Please describe any public benefits the system is expected to have.
This system will optimize the inspection procedures by reducing processing
times, streamlining the inspection/assessment process, saving costs on an
otherwise time consuming process. The solution will increase the ability of
the department to ensure the integrity of the program.
4. How effective will the system likely be in meeting client needs?
Moderately effective [ Points: +1 ]
5. Please describe any improvements, benefits, or advantages you expect from using an
automated system. This could include relevant program indicators and performance targets.
The solution will increase the ability of the department to ensure the integrity
of the program. The system would be able to efficiently categorize cases
based on a number of criteria including (1) length of recipient’s absence from
Canada, (2) state of recipient (alive or deceased), (3) status of recipient’s OAS
benefts (i.e.: active, ineligible, etc), highlighting the ones requiring inspection.
The system will support agents by providing them with a list of cases that
require immediate action by filtering the cases based on the set program
criteria and grouping them.
6. Please describe how you will ensure that the system is confined to addressing the client
needs identified above.
This system will not autonomously make decisions. Instead, it will use the
program criteria to categorize the cases based on priority. The Integrity
Operations agent would then conduct a thorough pre-assessment before
beginning an inspection.
7. Please describe any trade-offs between client interests and program objectives that you have
considered during the design of the project.
The purpose of this system is to enhance the integrity of the OAS program by
supporting agents in making decisions around the eligibility of clients
receiving OAS (and other non-portable benefits such as GIS). To ensure that
this system does not disproportionately target at-risk clients, the system will
undergo an ethical review and fairness assessment.
8. Have alternative non-automated processes been considered?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
9. If non-automated processes were considered, why was automation identified as the preferred
option?
This solution will enhance the integrity of the program by optimizing the
process and delivering improved outcomes. The solution will increase
efficiencies by lowering resource costs, reducing processing times, improving
accuracy of benefits distribution by identifying ineligible recipients, and
thereby improve stewardship of the program’s financial integrity.
10. What would be the consequence of not deploying the system?
Service quality is not as high
Risk Profile
11. Is the project within an area of intense public scrutiny (e.g. because of privacy concerns)
and/or frequent litigation?
No [ Points: +0 ]
12. Are clients in this line of business particularly vulnerable?
Yes [ Points: +3 ]
13. Are stakes of the decisions very high?
No [ Points: +0 ]
14. Will this project have major impacts on staff, either in terms of their numbers or their roles?
No [ Points: +0 ]
15. Will the use of the system create or exacerbate barriers for persons with disabilities?
No [ Points: +0 ]
Project Authority
16. Will you require new policy authority for this project?
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Algorithm
17. The algorithm used will be a (trade) secret
No [ Points: +0 ]
18. The algorithmic process will be difficult to interpret or to explain
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Decision
19. Please describe the decision(s) that will be automated.
The system will filter the data based on the program criteria and summarize
the data into groups with a grouping label and provide a summary by group.
This will help facilitate the prioritization of cases that need immediate action.
20. Does the decision pertain to any of the categories below (check all that apply):
Social assistance (employment insurance, disability claims) [ Points: +1 ]
Impact Assessment
21. Which of the following best describes the type of automation you are planning?
Partial automation (the system will contribute to administrative decision-
making by supporting an officer through assessments, recommendations,
intermediate decisions, or other outputs) [ Points: +2 ]
22. Please describe the role of the system in the decision-making process.
The system will not make any decisions autonomously. Instead, it will use the
program criteria to categorize the cases based on priority. The Integrity
Operations agent will then conduct a thorough pre-assessment before
beginning an inspection.
23. Will the system be making decisions or assessments that require judgement or discretion?
No [ Points: +0 ]
24. Please describe the criteria used to evaluate client data and the operations applied to
process it.
The criteria for review are: (1) length of recipient’s absence from Canada, (2)
state of recipient (alive or deceased), (3) status of recipient’s OAS benefits
(i.e.: active, ineligible, etc)
25. Please describe the output produced by the system and any relevant information needed to
interpret it in the context of the administrative decision.
The system will output filtered observations that are labelled and summarized
by group. This will support Integrity Operations agents to identify and address
the high priority cases which require verification of continued eligibility for
OAS and additional supplemental benefits. An administrative decision that an
OAS recipient is no longer eligible to receive benefits payments will be
supported by the system’s outputs which identify the cases that are high
priority and require immediate assessment. Before each inspection there is a
manual pre-assessment by a human to ensure the eligibility criteria for
inspection have been met and the entire inspection process then on is
conducted by a human.
26. Will the system perform an assessment or other operation that would not otherwise be
completed by a human?
No [ Points: +0 ]
27. Is the system used by a different part of the organization than the ones who developed it?
No [ Points: +0 ]
28. Are the impacts resulting from the decision reversible?
Reversible [ Points: +1 ]
29. How long will impacts from the decision last?
Impacts are most likely to be brief [ Points: +1 ]
30. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The system will not render decisions. Instead, it supports Integrity Operations
agents’ decision making by prioritizing cases which will then be thoroughly
inspected by the agents. Once an agent makes a determination that an
individual is no longer eligible to receive OAS benefits, the individual has
recourse whereby they may request a reconsideration of the decision under s.
27.1 of the OAS Act.
31. The impacts that the decision will have on the rights or freedoms of individuals will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
32. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The system does not render decisions regarding the recipient’s eligibility.
33. The impacts that the decision will have on the equality, dignity, privacy, and autonomy of
individuals will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
34. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
Although the system will produce filtered observations that are labelled and
summarized by group, the decision to proceed with an inspection and all
related decisions are made at the Integrity Operations agents’ discretion.
However, if the system were to malfunction, there is a risk it could
inadvertently overrepresent at-risk recipients.
35. The impacts that the decision will have on the health and well-being of individuals will likely
be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
36. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The system does not render decisions regarding the recipient’s eligibility. The
cases are thoroughly investigated by Integrity Operations agents who
determine the recipient’s continued eligibility.
37. The impacts that the decision will have on the economic interests of individuals will likely
be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
38. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The system will not render decisions regarding the recipient’s eligibility. The
high risk cases that are identified by the system will be thoroughly assessed
by Integrity Operations agents who determine the recipient’s continued
eligibility.
39. The impacts that the decision will have on the ongoing sustainability of an environmental
ecosystem, will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
40. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
N/A
About the Data - A. Data Source
41. Will the Automated Decision System use personal information as input data?
Yes [ Points: +4 ]
42. Have you verified that the use of personal information is limited to only what is directly
related to delivering a program or service?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
43. Is the personal information of individuals being used in a decision-making process that
directly affects those individuals?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
44. Have you verified if the system is using personal information in a way that is consistent
with: (a) the current Personal Information Banks (PIBs) and Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs)
of your programs or (b) planned or implemented modifications to the PIBs or PIAs that take new
uses and processes into account?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
45. Please list relevant PIB Bank Numbers.
CBSA PPU 1202, ESDC PPU 116, ESDC PPU 336, ESDC PPU 390
46. What is the highest security classification of the input data used by the system? (Select one)
Protected B / Protected C [ Points: +3 ]
47. Who controls the data?
Federal government [ Points: +1 ]
48. Will the system use data from multiple different sources?
Yes [ Points: +4 ]
49. Will the system require input data from an Internet- or telephony-connected device? (e.g.
Internet of Things, sensor)
No [ Points: +0 ]
50. Will the system interface with other IT systems?
No [ Points: +0 ]
51. Who collected the data used for training the system?
Your institution [ Points: +1 ]
52. Who collected the input data used by the system?
Another federal institution [ Points: +2 ]
53. Please describe the input data collected and used by the system, its source, and method of
collection.
CBSA: Data used to identify the recipient’s length of stay outside Canada
ESDC: Recipient’s personal and OAS and eligibility information
About the Data - B. Type of Data
54. Will the system require the analysis of unstructured data to render a recommendation or a
decision?
No [ Points: 0 ]
Section 3.2: Mitigation Questions and Answers
Consultations
1. Internal Stakeholders (federal institutions, including the federal public service)
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
2. Which Internal Stakeholders have you engaged?
Data Governance
Legal Services
Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada
Other (describe)
3. Please describe
Chief Data Officer Branch, Privacy Management Division, Artificial Intelligence
Centre of Expertise, Legal Services Unit, Office of the Privacy Commissioner of
Canada. The Chief Data Officer Branch provided data governance guidance.
The Privacy Management Division provided guidance on the completion of the
Privacy Impact Assessment. The Artificial Intelligence Centre of Expertise
provided guidance on the completion of the DADM, Legal Services provided a
legal risk assessment of the AI solution, and consultations with the Office of
the Privacy Commissioner provided guidance on the protection of personal
information.
4. External Stakeholders (groups in other sectors or jurisdictions)
No [ Points: +0 ]
De-Risking and Mitigation Measures - Data Quality
5. Do you have documented processes in place to test datasets against biases and other
unexpected outcomes? This could include experience in applying frameworks, methods,

No [ Points: +0 ]

Yes [ Points: +2 ]

27. Is there a recourse process established for clients that wish to challenge the decision?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
28. Does the system enable human override of system decisions?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
29. Is there a process in place to log the instances when overrides were performed?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
30. Does the system's audit trail include change control processes to record modifications to the
system's operation or performance?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
31. Have you prepared a concept case to the Government of Canada Enterprise Architecture
Review Board?
No [ Points: +0 ]
De-Risking and Mitigation Measures - Privacy
32. If your system uses or creates personal information, have you undertaken a Privacy Impact
Assessment, or updated an existing one?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
33. Please indicate the following in your answer: Title and scope of the Privacy Impact
Assessment; How the automation project fits into the program; and Date of Privacy Impact
Assessment completion or modification.
Privacy Impact Assessment for Receipt of Entry-Exit Data from the Canada
Border Service Agency by the Old Age Security Program.
The automation project fits into the prioritization and optimization of
observations, as described in the business flow section of the Privacy Impact
Assessment.
Date of completion 30 Jul 2020.
34. Have you designed and built security and privacy into your systems from the concept stage
of the project?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
35. Is the information used within a closed system (i.e. no connections to the Internet, Intranet
or any other system)?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
36. If the sharing of personal information is involved, has an agreement or arrangement with
appropriate safeguards been established?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
37. Will you de-identify any personal information used or created by the system at any point in
the lifecycle?
No [ Points: +0 ]
